---
title: "Getting Started"
subtitle: "The CS112 Oracle"
# date: "Week of Aug 25, 2026"
---

::: {.callout-note}
**This pre-lab is not graded.** It exists so that you arrive at your first lab session with your tools already set up and working. Please complete it *before* your first lab meeting. If you get stuck, post on Moodle or ask a classmate — everyone is figuring this out together.

**Plan to set aside about 1.5 to 2 hours.** Most of that time goes to the one-time setup steps — Coder, VS Code, and GitHub. Once those are done, they're done for the whole semester. The actual coding at the end is short.
:::

## Objectives

By the end of this pre-lab, you will be able to:

- Log in to Coder and use a Linux terminal.
- Use basic Linux commands (`mkdir`, `cd`, `ls`, `pwd`).
- Connect VS Code on your laptop to the Calvin Linux environment.
- Create a GitHub account and accept a GitHub Classroom assignment.
- Clone a repository and open it in VS Code.
- Understand what a makefile does and how compilation works in C++.
- Declare variables of type `string` and `int` in C++.
- Print output to the screen using `cout` and read input from the keyboard using `cin`.
- Call a function that takes parameters and returns a value.
- Compile and run your code, then push your changes to GitHub.

## 1. Introduction

**Welcome to CS112!**

If you are coming from CS108, you are used to Python — a language where you just run `python main.py` and things happen. C++ works differently. Before a C++ program can run, it must be **compiled**: translated from human-readable source code into machine instructions that the CPU can execute directly. This makes C++ programs much faster than Python, but it adds a step to your workflow.

In this course, we will write C++ using **Visual Studio Code** (VS Code) as our editor, but we will run and compile our programs on **Calvin's Linux servers** through a service called **Coder**. This means your editor lives on your laptop, but the C++ compiler lives on the server.

Here is a quick look at how Python and C++ differ before you run anything:

| | Python | C++ |
|---|---|---|
| How you run it | `python main.py` (interpreted) | `./a00` (compiled first) |
| Output to screen | `print("Hello")` | `cout << "Hello" << endl;` |
| Input from keyboard | `name = input("Name? ")` | `cin >> name;` |
| Variable types | Figured out automatically | You must declare them |
| End of a statement | Just a newline | Semicolon `;` |
| Grouping code | Indentation | Curly braces `{ }` |

You will notice these differences throughout the pre-lab. We will point them out as we go.

## 2. Using Linux

All your CS112 work will run on a **Linux** server. Linux is a Unix-based operating system used extensively in computer science, software engineering, and research. Learning your way around a Linux terminal is a genuine career skill.

Here are the commands you will use most often:

| Command | What it does |
|---|---|
| `pwd` | **P**rint **W**orking **D**irectory — shows where you are |
| `ls` | **L**i**s**t — shows files and folders here |
| `cd dirname` | **C**hange **D**irectory — move into `dirname` |
| `cd ..` | Go up one directory level |
| `mkdir dirname` | **M**a**k**e **Dir**ectory — create a new folder |

::: {.callout-tip}
If you want to go deeper, [Ubuntu's command-line tutorial](https://ubuntu.com/tutorials/command-line-for-beginners#1-overview){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} is a great free resource to work through on your own time.
:::

## 3. Coder

The Calvin CS Department provides a service called [Coder](https://coder.cs.calvin.edu){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} that gives you access to a Linux environment and a C++ compiler. You do not need to install a compiler on your own laptop — Coder handles that.

Do this:

1. Open [https://coder.cs.calvin.edu](https://coder.cs.calvin.edu){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} in your browser.
2. Sign in using your **full Calvin email address** (e.g., `jcalvin@calvin.edu`).
3. Click **New Workspace** → **Calvin CS Linux**.
4. Name it `cs112`. Leave all other options at their defaults. Click **Create Workspace**.
5. Wait a few seconds for your workspace to start. Leave this browser tab open.

## 4. Where to Store Your CS112 Work

You will have many assignments in CS112. It is good practice to keep them organized under one folder. Let us create that folder now.

From your Coder webpage, click the **Terminal** button. Then type:

```bash
mkdir cs112
```

Change into that directory:

```bash
cd cs112
```

Verify where you are:

```bash
pwd
```

You should see `/home/<username>/cs112`, where `<username>` is your Calvin username. Every assignment you clone this semester will go inside this `cs112` folder.

## 5. Installing and Connecting VS Code

We will edit code on your laptop using VS Code, but compile and run it on the Linux server through Coder.

**Step 1 — Install VS Code on your laptop:**

1. Go to [https://code.visualstudio.com](https://code.visualstudio.com){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} and download VS Code for your operating system. Install it.
2. Open VS Code.

**Step 2 — Connect VS Code to Coder:**

3. Return to the Coder browser tab. Find the **VS Code Desktop** button and click it.
4. When VS Code asks if you want to install the **Coder** extension, accept. This allows VS Code on your laptop to reach the compiler on the server.
5. VS Code will ask you to select the platform for the remote host — choose **Linux**.
6. Once connected, choose **Open Folder** and type `/home/<username>/cs112` (substituting your actual username). Click OK.
7. If asked to trust the authors, select **Yes, I trust the authors**. (You are the author.)
8. Open a terminal inside VS Code: **Terminal menu → New Terminal**. You should see a Linux terminal at the bottom of the screen — this is running on the Calvin server.

::: {.callout-tip}
**Shortcut:** In VS Code, press **Ctrl+`** to open (or toggle) the integrated terminal. On some keyboard layouts, if the backtick key is awkward to find, use **Terminal menu → New Terminal** instead.
:::

## 6. Configuring VS Code

A few one-time settings will make your life much easier all semester.

**Install the C++ extension:**

Open any `.cpp` file. VS Code will suggest installing the **C/C++ Extension Pack** in the lower-right corner. Accept it. If it does not appear automatically, open the Extensions panel (left sidebar), search for `C/C++ Extension Pack`, and install it.

**Configure Auto Save:**

Press **F1** (or Ctrl+Shift+P), type `Preferences: Open User Settings`, and select it. In the search bar at the top, type `Auto Save`. Set `Files: Auto Save` to **onFocusChange**. This saves your file automatically whenever you switch to another window — no more losing work because you forgot to save.

**Set Tab Size:**

In the same Settings page, search for `Tab Size`. Set `Editor: Tab Size` to **4**.

**Set C++ Formatting Style:**

Under **Extensions → C/C++ → Formatting**, find `C_Cpp: Clang_format_style` and set it to:

```
{ BasedOnStyle: Google, IndentWidth: 4, AccessModifierOffset: -4, AllowShortFunctionsOnASingleLine: InlineOnly }
```

**Set Intellisense to use g++:**

Press Ctrl+Shift+P, search for `C/C++: Select IntelliSense Configuration`, and choose **g++ (found in /usr/bin)**. This tells VS Code which compiler to use when underlining errors in your editor.

### Turn Off GitHub Copilot for This Course

In CS112, you are learning programming fundamentals from the beginning. Keep **GitHub Copilot turned off** while doing course work so you practice writing and debugging your own code instead of accepting auto-generated code.

Copilot can auto-fill full lines or whole functions. That may feel helpful in the moment, but it can slow down real learning when you are building core skills.

To turn Copilot off in VS Code for this class:

1. Open the **Extensions** view in VS Code.
2. Search for **GitHub Copilot Chat**.
3. Click the gear icon on the extension.
4. Choose **Disable AI Features (Workspace)** for your CS112 folder.

If you want it off everywhere in VS Code, choose **Disable AI Features** instead.

You can also use the **Copilot button in the bottom-right corner** of VS Code and choose either **Disable AI Features (Workspace)** or **Disable AI Features**.

You can re-enable it later by returning to the extension and clicking **Enable**.

## 7. Create a GitHub Account

GitHub is where all your CS112 code will live. Your grader will visit your GitHub repository to read and grade your code. It also means you can access your work from any machine.

If you do not already have an account:

1. Go to [https://github.com](https://github.com){target="_blank" rel="noopener"} and sign up. Use your **Calvin email address**.
2. Complete the verification steps (you may need to set up two-factor authentication).
3. Skip any personalization steps — just click "Skip Personalization" at the bottom.

Keep your GitHub tab open for the next step.

## 8. Accept the GitHub Classroom Assignment and Clone Your Repo

Each assignment in CS112 is distributed through **GitHub Classroom**. When you accept an invitation, GitHub automatically creates a private repository just for you with the starter code already inside.

::: {.callout-important}
Get the **Pre-Lab 00 GitHub Classroom invitation link from Moodle** and open it there.

All CS112 Classroom invitation links are distributed through Moodle so only Calvin students can access and accept assignments.

After you accept, wait a few seconds and refresh until it says "You're ready to go!" Then click the repository link GitHub provides.
:::

**Get the repo URL:**

On your repository page, click the green **\<\> Code** button, then the **SSH** tab. Click the copy icon to copy the URL (it starts with `git@github.com:...`).

**Clone the repo:**

In your VS Code terminal (the one connected to Coder), make sure you are in your `cs112` folder:

```bash
pwd
```

If you are not, run `cd ~/cs112`. Then clone:

```bash
git clone <paste-your-repo-url-here>
```

If this is your first time using Git with Coder, you will see an error about SSH keys. Read the next section.

### Setting Up SSH Keys for GitHub

GitHub requires an SSH key to verify your identity. Coder generates one for you automatically. When `git clone` fails, look at the error output — somewhere near the top you will see a public key that looks like:

```
ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAA...
```

Copy that entire line. Then:

1. Go to [https://github.com/settings/keys](https://github.com/settings/keys){target="_blank" rel="noopener"}.
2. Click **New SSH Key**.
3. Title: `CS112 Coder`
4. Paste the key into the Key field. Click **Add SSH Key**.

Now retry the clone:

```bash
git clone <paste-your-repo-url-here>
```

This time it should succeed. Verify by listing your files:

```bash
ls
```

You should see a directory named something like `a00-yourgithubusername`. Change into it:

```bash
cd a00-yourgithubusername
ls
```

You will find: `main.cpp`, `fortune.h`, `fortune.cpp`, `makefile`, and `README.md`.

Open this folder in VS Code: **File → Open Folder**, then navigate to `/home/<username>/cs112/a00-yourgithubusername`.

## 9. Understanding the Makefile

Before you compile anything, let us understand *how* compilation works. This is one of the biggest conceptual differences between Python and C++.

### How Python runs your code

In Python, you type `python main.py` and the Python **interpreter** reads your source code line by line and executes it. Your `.py` file is never turned into anything else — the interpreter does all the work at runtime.

### How C++ compilation works

In C++, your source code goes through **two stages** before it can run:

**Stage 1 — Compile:** Each `.cpp` source file is individually translated into an **object file** (`.o`). Object files contain machine code, but they are not yet runnable — they might refer to functions defined in other files.

**Stage 2 — Link:** The **linker** combines all the `.o` files into a single **executable** (in our case, a file called `a00`). This is the program you actually run.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
	A[main.cpp] -->|compile| B[main.o]
	C[fortune.cpp] -->|compile| D[fortune.o]
	B -->|link| E[a00]
	D -->|link| E
	E -->|run| F[output]
```

Doing this manually every time would look like:

```bash
g++ -Wall -std=c++17 -c main.cpp        # produces main.o
g++ -Wall -std=c++17 -c fortune.cpp     # produces fortune.o
g++ -Wall -std=c++17 -o a00 main.o fortune.o  # produces a00
```

That is three commands, and if you only changed `fortune.cpp`, you would still have to remember which commands to re-run. A **makefile** automates this.

### Reading the makefile

Open `makefile` in VS Code. Here is what each part does:

```makefile
CXX      = g++
CXXFLAGS = -Wall -std=c++17
```

These are **variables**. `CXX` is the compiler name. `CXXFLAGS` are the options we always pass to it: `-Wall` turns on all warnings (helpful!), `-std=c++17` selects the C++17 standard.

```makefile
a00: main.o fortune.o
	$(CXX) $(CXXFLAGS) -o a00 main.o fortune.o
```

This is a **rule**. The format is:

```
target: dependencies
    command
```

`a00` is what we want to build. It depends on `main.o` and `fortune.o`. If either of those is out of date, `make` runs the command to rebuild `a00`. Notice the command line starts with a **tab character** — makefiles require this, not spaces.

```makefile
main.o: main.cpp fortune.h
	$(CXX) $(CXXFLAGS) -c main.cpp
```

This rule says: to build `main.o`, compile `main.cpp` (the `-c` flag means "compile only, don't link yet"). It depends on `fortune.h` too — if the header changes, `main.o` needs to be rebuilt.

```makefile
fortune.o: fortune.cpp fortune.h
	$(CXX) $(CXXFLAGS) -c fortune.cpp
```

Same idea for `fortune.cpp`.

```makefile
clean:
	rm -f *.o a00
```

`make clean` deletes all object files and the executable. Use this when you want a completely fresh build.

### `make` is smart about what to rebuild

One of `make`'s best features: it only recompiles files that have changed since the last build. If you only edit `fortune.cpp`, `make` will recompile `fortune.o` and re-link `a00` — but it will *not* recompile `main.cpp`, because `main.cpp` has not changed. This saves time on larger projects.

## 10. Compile and Run the Starter Code

In your VS Code terminal, make sure you are inside your `a00-yourgithubusername` directory. Then type:

```bash
make
```

You should see output like:

```
g++ -Wall -std=c++17 -c main.cpp
g++ -Wall -std=c++17 -c fortune.cpp
g++ -Wall -std=c++17 -o a00 main.o fortune.o
```

Type `ls` — you should now see `a00` in the directory. Run it:

```bash
./a00
```

The `./` means "run this file in the current directory." The program will immediately exit without doing much — that is expected. `main.cpp` has placeholders where you will write the interactive code. That is your task in the next section.

## 11. Your Coding Task: Complete `main.cpp`

Open `main.cpp` in VS Code. This is the only file you will edit. `fortune.cpp` and `fortune.h` are already complete — read through them to get a sense of how a C++ function is written, but do not change them.

`main.cpp` has four TODO comments. Work through them in order.

---

### TODO 1 — Declare your variables

In Python, you just assign a value and the language figures out the type:

```python
name = ""
stress_level = 0
birth_month = 0
```

In C++, you must declare the **type** of each variable before you use it:

```cpp
string name;
int stressLevel;
int birthMonth;
```

`string` holds text. `int` holds a whole number. Notice the semicolon at the end of each line — every statement in C++ ends with one.

Add these three declarations where TODO 1 is.

---

### TODO 2 — Print prompts and read input

Use `cout` to print a line to the screen and `cin` to read input from the keyboard.

**Printing with `cout`:**

```cpp
cout << "What is your name? ";
```

The `<<` operator sends text to the output stream. Note there is **no** `endl` here — we want the cursor to stay on the same line so the user types right after the prompt.

**Reading with `cin`:**

```cpp
cin >> name;
```

The `>>` operator reads one word (or number) from the keyboard and stores it in the variable.

In Python this would be:

```python
name = input("What is your name? ")
```

In C++, the prompt and the read are two separate statements. Write one `cout` and one `cin` for each of the three pieces of information.

Use these exact prompt strings (the autograder will check them):

```
"What is your name? "
"On a scale of 1-5, how nervous are you about CS112? "
"What month were you born in (1-12)? "
```

---

### TODO 3 — Call `getFortune()`

`getFortune()` is a function declared in `fortune.h`. It takes two `int` parameters and returns a `string`. Call it like this:

```cpp
string fortune = getFortune(stressLevel, birthMonth);
```

This creates a new `string` variable named `fortune` and stores the result of the function call in it. Add this line where TODO 3 is.

---

### TODO 4 — Print the Oracle's response

Use `cout` to print two lines inside the banner:

```
  The Oracle speaks, NAME!
```
*(an empty line)*
```
FORTUNE
```

Where `NAME` and `FORTUNE` are your variables. In C++, you can chain multiple things on one `cout` line:

```cpp
cout << "  The Oracle speaks, " << name << "!" << endl;
```

---

### Compile and test

After filling in all four TODOs, compile:

```bash
make
```

If you see errors, read them carefully — the compiler tells you the file name and line number of the problem. Fix the error and `make` again.

Run the program:

```bash
./a00
```

Enter your name, a stress level between 1 and 5, and your birth month. You should see the full Oracle experience, including a fortune at the end.

Once it looks right, run the autograder:

```bash
make test
```

You will see a PASS or FAIL for each test case, with hints if something is wrong. Fix any failures and run `make test` again until all tests pass.

::: {.callout-note}
**What about `make clean`?**

If edits do not seem to take effect, try:

```bash
make clean
make
```

This forces a completely fresh build.
:::

## 12. Push Your Code to GitHub

When all tests pass, submit your work to GitHub.

::: {.callout-important}
Add your name to the `README.md` file before pushing. Open it in VS Code, add a line with your name, and save.
:::

In VS Code, look at the **Source Control** icon in the left sidebar (it looks like a branch). Click it. You will see your changed files listed. Type a short commit message (e.g., `"Complete main.cpp"`), then click the **checkmark** to commit. Follow any prompts to sync/push to your remote repository.

To verify the push worked, go to your repository page on github.com and confirm you can see your updated `main.cpp`.

## You are ready!

Congratulations — your development environment is set up and you have written your first C++ code for CS112. See you in lab!

::: {.callout-tip}
**Working from home?** As long as you have VS Code and can connect to Coder, you can do all your CS112 work from your laptop anywhere with internet. The setup you did today is all you need.
:::
